feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

paragraphs.text: Comments on the planned Digital Justice Act (hereinafter DFA)
In our comments, we focus in particular on addressing problematic personalisation practices, including targeting consumers' vulnerabilities for personalised advertising and pricing.

The protection of user data is key in our view: It is unacceptable and in possible breach of EU legislation to make participation on social networks conditional only on regular payment or consent to the use of personal and sensitive data for targeted advertising, without the possibility of an untargeted alternative of displaying an advertisement. In particular, when the right of access to social networks can be inferred as a necessary condition for the exercise of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights, guaranteed rights to freedom of expression as well as the right to information, as well as the right to privacy and family life. Commission Staff Working Document Fitness Check of EU consumer law on digital fairness of 3. 10. 2024, p. 27 states: "the 2023 CCS study (...) found that 70 % of consumers are concerned about how their personal data is used and shared, representing an increase of 21 percentage points compared to 2018".

As described by the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU): "The Facebook social network business model is based on funding through online advertising tailored to individual users of the social network, in particular according to their consumer habits, their interests, their purchasing power and their personal circumstances. That advertising is made possible in technical terms by the automated production of detailed profiles in respect of the network users and the users of the online services offered at the level of the Meta group. To that end, in addition to the data provided by the users directly when they sign up for the online services concerned, other user- and device-related data are also collected on and off that social network and the online services provided by the Meta group, and linked to their various user accounts. These data, taken as a whole, make it possible to draw detailed conclusions on the preferences and interests of these users."1

The alternative offered by Facebook, for example, is a direct payment for the service provided. Therefore, a clear message from Facebook to us reads: Either you will pay for yourself and your behaviour, or you will pay directly and we will be satisfied with the data from the others.

In its non-binding opinion, the European Data Protection Board interpreted such a practice as being contrary to Regulation 2016/679 of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data (GDPR)2. Because consumers do not give their consent freely when they have a paid version as the only alternative. According to the Authority, consent would be free only if other free alternatives were available without behavioural advertising, e.g. with a form of advertising involving the processing of minor amounts of personal data (or without processing personal data), such as contextual advertising, advertising targeting topics selected by the user or general advertising. Meta brought an action before the General3Court, seeking annulment of

1 Paragraph 27 of the judgment of the Grand Chamber of the CJEU, Meta Platforms Inc. and Others v Bundeskartellamt, 4.7.2023, ref.: C-252/21 2https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/CS/ALL/?uri=celex:32016R0679 3order of the General Court, Meta Platforms Ireland Ltd v European Data Protection Board dated: 29.4.2025, Sp. ref./Ref.: T-319/24

the Opinion and compensation for the alleged damage. The General Court dismissed the action as, in part, inadmissible and, in part, unfounded in law: The Authority's opinion is not legally binding and cannot in itself directly cause damage to Met. Network operators are resisting when their business is trying to set borders by the EU and Member States in order to save their inhabitants from forced accession to unilaterally determined trading conditions. At the moment, 4 Meta on Facebook also offers a version with less personalised ads, which, however, still uses data on how consumers react to advertisements, age, gender and location, information about consumers' devices, such as the type of device and browser used, as well as information on the content viewed by consumers. There is no unpaid version without personalisation contrary to existing EU law.

We consider it unacceptable that users should only be forced to choose between a paid version and a personalisation version when accessing social networks. Personalisation should be a mere 'advantage' offered, which can be totally rejected, and even so, access free of charge, which is conditional, at most, on the display of advertising without personalisation. We recommend that the above be clearly defined in relation to the GDPR in the new DFA.
